analogy: Think of "Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes" through the chapter's own opening images: leisure opportunities s/he avails, the health access s/he has, i.e. her/his lifestyle in general. As in the case of social structure, social stratification constrains individual action. One of the central concerns of the sociological perspective has been to understand the dialectical relationship between the individual... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that leisure opportunities s/he avails, the health access s/he has, i.e. her/his lifestyle in general.

The text explains that as in the case of social structure, social stratification constrains individual action.

The text explains that one of the central concerns of the sociological perspective has been to understand the dialectical relationship between the individual and society.

The text explains that you will recall C.Wright Mill’s elaboration of the sociological imagination that seeks to unfold the interplay between an individual’s biography and society’s history.

The text explains that it is towards understanding this dialectical relationship between the society and individual that we need to discuss the three central concepts of structure, stratification and social processes in this chapter.

The text explains that in the next few chapters we then move on to how social structure in rural and urban societies are different, to broader relationships between environment and society.

The text explains that in the last two chapters we look at western social thinkers and Indian sociologists and their writings that would help us further understand the ideas of social structure, stratification as well as social processes.

The text explains that cHAPTER 1 SOCIAL STRUCTURE, STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL PROCESSES IN SOCIETY INTRODUCTION You will recall that the earlier book Introducing Sociology, Class XI had begun with a discussion on the relationship between personal problems and social issues.

The text explains that we also saw how individuals are located within collectivities such as groups, classes, gender, castes and tribes.

The text explains that indeed each of you, is a member of not just one kind of collectivity, but many overlapping ones.

The text explains that for instance, you are a member of your own peer group, your family and kin, your class and gender, your country and region.

The text explains that each individual thus has a specific location in the social structure and social stratification system (see pages 28-35 in Introducing Sociology).

The text explains that this also implies that they have different levels and types of access to social resources.

The text explains that in other words the choices an individual has in life in terms of the school s/he goes to — or if s/he goes to school at all — would depend on the social s t r a t u m t h a t s/ h e b e l o n g s t o .

The text explains that likewise with the clothes s/he gets to wear, the food s/he consumes, the

The text explains that 2 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY regularities that the concept of social structure refers.

The text explains that source: Durkheim, Emile,1933, The Division of Labour in Society, pp.50-1, A Free Press Paperback, The MacMillan Company, New York.

The text explains that the opportunities and resources available to individuals and groups to engage in competition, cooperation or conflict are shaped by social structure and social stratification.

The text explains that by producing their means of subsistence men are indirectly producing their material life (Marx 1972:37).

The text explains that however, he felt that though modern competition ‘is described as the fight of all against all, but at the same time it is the fight for all’; this in the sense that economic competition is directed toward maximum output at minimum cost.

The text explains that this ideology assumes that individuals compete on an equal basis, i.e. that all individuals are positioned equally in the competition for education, jobs, or resources.

The text explains that we have already seen how conflict theorists believe that scarcity of resources in society produces conflict as groups struggle to gain access to and control over those resources.

The text explains that source: Srinivas, M.N., 1972, Social Change in Modern India, pp.159-160, Orient Longman, New Delhi.

The text explains that for example, the existence of a peasant movement is an overt ex- pression of a deep rooted conflict over land resources.

The text explains that material pressures and incentives to cooperate extend to distribution and there is little evidence of overt conflict over distributional processes.

The text explains that denied access to extra- household relationships and resources, it is in their material interests to subscribe to the general son-preference which characterises this culture, and they invest in a great deal of ‘selfless’ devotion in order to win their sons as allies and insurance against an uncertain future. ‘Maternal altruism’ in the northern Indian plain is likely to be biased towards sons and can be seen as women’s response to patriarchal risk.

The text explains that identify the different social groups within it and notice the role of power and access to resources. the police officials.

The text explains that source: Oommen, T.K., 1972: Charisma, Stability and Change; An Analysis of Bhoodan-Gramdan Movement in India, p.84.

The text explains that source: Oommen, T.K., 1972, Charisma, Stability and Change; An Analysis of Bhoodan-Gramdan Movement in India, p.88.

The text explains that structures: Refers generally to constructed frameworks and patterns of organisation, which in some way constrain or direct human behaviour.

The text explains that as the division of labour becomes more complex, people become more and more dependent on one another, because each person needs goods and services that those in other occupations supply.

The text explains that relationships of economic reciprocity and mutual dependency come to replace shared beliefs in creating social consensus.

The text explains that social Constraint: A term referring to the fact that the groups and societies of which we are a part exert a conditioning influence on our behaviour.

The text explains that social constraint was regarded by Durkheim as one of the distinctive properties of ‘social facts’.

The text explains that a Free Press (Paperback), The MacMillan Company, New York.

The text explains that controversies in Sociology: A Canadian Introduction.

The text explains that 1990. “Gender and Cooperative Conflicts” in Persistent Inequalities (ed) II.Tinker, pp.123-49.

The text explains that charisma, Stability and Change; An Analysis of Bhoodan- Gramdan Movement in India.

The text explains that arguing With the Crocodile, Gender and Class in Bangladesh, Zed Books, London.

cases.point: Each individual thus has a specific location in the social structure and social stratification system (see pages 28-35 in Introducing Sociology). (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 1)

cases.name: You
cases.point: You have already discussed social stratification in some detail in Chapter 2 of the earlier book Introducing Sociology, Class XI (NCERT, 2006). (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 1)

cases.name: Emile Durkheim
cases.point: A major theme pursued by Emile Durkheim (and by many other sociologists since) is that the societies exert social constraint over the actions of their members. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: Even
schemes.point: Even as old members of the family or school may pass away and new members enter, the institution goes on. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 1)

schemes.name: Do
schemes.point: Do social structure and social stratification influence the manner people act? (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 1)

schemes.name: Other
schemes.point: Other social thinkers like Karl Marx would emphasise the constraints of social structure but would at the same time stress human creativity or agency to both... (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: Do
schemes.point: Do students, teachers and the staff have to act in certain ways to maintain or reproduce the structure? (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: At
schemes.point: At the same time, humans do act to modify the structure and system of stratification that exists. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch1 pg 5)

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER 1 and SOCIAL STRUCTURE, STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER 1 and SOCIAL STRUCTURE, STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Social Structure Stratification and Social Processes, which statement is supported by the source text about 2 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY?
options: Upto a point, it is helpful to think of the structural characteristics of societies as resembling the structure of a building.
A building has walls, a floor and a roof, which together give it a particular ‘shape’ or form (Giddens 2004: 667).
But the metaphor can be a very misleading one if applied too strictly.
Social structures are made up of human actions and relationships.
